1.synthesis: Pharisees and scribes come from Jerusalem to challenge Jesus: "Why do your disciples break the tradition of the elders? For they do not wash their hands when they eat" (v.2). Jesus counters with a counter-charge: why do they break the commandment of God for the sake of their tradition? He gives a specific example — the Corban practice (korban: "gift dedicated to God") that allows a person to declare wealth promised to the Temple and thereby withhold it from aged parents who need financial support, nullifying the Fifth Commandment's provision for parents (vv.3–6). The exchange escalates to a prophetic indictment: Isaiah's word fits them exactly — "This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me" (vv.8–9, quoting Isaiah 29:13). Human tradition has not extended Torah; it has displaced it.
1.historical_context: The "tradition of the elders" (paradosis tōn presbyterōn) was the body of oral interpretation developed by the Pharisees to apply Mosaic law to every situation — later codified in the Mishnah. Handwashing before meals in this context was not hygienic but ritual: a purity practice to prevent defilement contracted from ordinary contact from passing into food. It was not Mosaic law but a later extension of the priestly purity requirements of Exodus 30:17–21 applied to lay Israelites. The Corban (qorbān) practice is attested in Josephus (Against Apion 1.167) and in Mark 7:11; rabbinic discussions confirm its use as a device to protect wealth from family claims. Isaiah 29:13 in the MT reads "their fear of me is a commandment of men"; the LXX (which Matthew uses) reads "in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" — Matthew's version fits exactly.
1.literary_structure: The conflict has a chiastic form: challenge (handwashing, v.2) → counter-challenge (Fifth Commandment, vv.3–6) → Isaiah citation (vv.7–9). The rhetorical move is tu quoque escalated: the Pharisees come with a specific complaint about a minor oral tradition; Jesus responds with a specific case of an oral tradition actively weaponized against a major written commandment. The Isaiah citation is not merely an insult but a structural claim: the Pharisees' pattern is not a recent deviation but the fulfillment of a prophetic diagnosis. The section leads directly into the teaching on true defilement (vv.10–20), which supplies the principle underlying the specific case.
1.theological_application: The Corban example names the precise danger of any system that multiplies religious obligation: rules designed to protect the law's spirit can become tools for circumventing it. A practice meant to honor God (dedicating goods to the Temple) becomes a mechanism to dishonor parents — whom God explicitly commanded be honored. The diagnostic of Isaiah 29:13 is permanent: lip-worship disconnected from heart-orientation is always empty, regardless of how elaborate or historically traditional the form. The question Jesus presses is not "what tradition do you follow?" but "does your practice produce obedience to what God actually commanded?"
1.christology: Jesus quotes Isaiah against the Jerusalem leadership's delegation — a move that places him in the prophetic tradition of those who called Israel back from tradition-covered disobedience to covenant-commanded reality. But his authority here exceeds Isaiah's: Isaiah reported what God said; Jesus judges which traditions are consistent with what God said and which nullify it. The phrase "you make void the word of God for the sake of your tradition" (v.6) implies that Jesus knows the word of God well enough to adjudicate between its authentic requirements and human additions — a claim to interpretive authority over the entire Mosaic tradition.

10.synthesis: Jesus calls the crowd and delivers a pithy teaching: "It is not what goes into the mouth that defiles a person, but what comes out of the mouth; this defiles a person" (v.11). The Pharisees are offended; Peter asks for explanation. Jesus clarifies in private: what enters the mouth goes through the digestive system and passes out; what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart, and it is from the heart that evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, and slander emerge — these defile (vv.17–20). The teaching implicitly relativizes ritual purity and radically elevates moral purity: the inside, not the outside, is where defilement originates.
10.historical_context: The purity system of Leviticus 11–15 and its Pharisaic extensions organized much of Jewish social life: who could eat with whom, what foods were permissible, what contact rendered one ritually unclean. Jesus's saying cuts across this entire system not by abolishing Torah directly but by relocating defilement from the ritual-physical domain to the moral-cardiac domain. The list of evils in vv.19–20 is a simplified Decalogue summary (murder, adultery, theft, false witness) plus sexual sin and slander — the interior vices the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:21–30) traced back to the heart. Mark's account adds the parenthetical note "thus he declared all foods clean" (Mark 7:19); Matthew omits it, perhaps reflecting a Jewish-Christian community still practicing dietary law but accepting the principle.
10.literary_structure: The teaching has a public/private double structure: principle announced to the crowd (v.11) → Pharisee reaction reported (vv.12–14) → disciples' request for explanation (v.15) → private explanation (vv.16–20). The private explanation is more radical than the public statement: it traces all moral evil to the heart — a claim that shifts the entire framework of purity from ritual to anthropological. The list of vices parallels the Ten Commandments' second table, suggesting that the "heart" Jesus is concerned with is specifically the moral self that either keeps or breaks the covenant commands. The Pharisees' being offended (v.12) is itself diagnostic: they are more concerned with the violation of their tradition than with the heart-defilement Jesus is naming.
10.theological_application: "Blind guides of the blind" (v.14) — Jesus's response to the news of the Pharisees' offense is not pastoral management but prophetic clarity. Some things said in truth will give offense, and the appropriate response is not to soften the teaching but to name what kind of plant the offended group is: "Every plant that my heavenly Father has not planted will be rooted up." The pastoral point for the disciples is the diagnostic principle: examine your heart (vv.17–19), not your rituals. The sins Jesus lists are not exotic vices but ordinary inner-life deformities — evil thoughts, the readiness for murder or adultery or theft — that are the real source of social and spiritual corruption.
10.christology: Jesus speaks about "my heavenly Father" (v.13) in the same breath as judging which plants will be rooted up: he is the one who knows what the Father has planted and what is human tradition masquerading as divine requirement. This claim presupposes an intimacy with the Father's will that enables him to sort authentic command from human addition — the same intimacy claimed in the Sermon on the Mount's "you have heard ... but I say to you" formula. The one who redefines defilement from outside to inside is the one who will, at the Last Supper, speak of his own blood as the covenant — the purest possible contact, redefining what makes a person clean from within.

21.synthesis: Jesus withdraws to the district of Tyre and Sidon, and a Canaanite woman — a Gentile — cries after him: "Have mercy on me, O Lord, Son of David; my daughter is severely oppressed by a demon" (v.22). Jesus does not answer; the disciples ask him to send her away; Jesus explains that he was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (v.24). She kneels and begs; he says it is not right to take children's bread and throw it to the dogs (v.26). She answers: "Yes, Lord, yet even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters' table" (v.27). Jesus marvels and heals her daughter immediately, calling her faith "great" (v.28). The episode stands in deliberate tension with the mission restriction of Matthew 10:5–6, pointing toward the post-resurrection universal commission.
21.historical_context: Tyre and Sidon were Phoenician coastal cities north of Galilee, mentioned by Isaiah and Ezekiel as types of prideful Gentile power (Isaiah 23; Ezekiel 26–28); Jesus cited them as models of potential repentance in Matthew 11:21. The term "Canaanite" (Chananaios) is Matthew's distinctive choice over Mark's "Syrophoenician" — an archaic OT term that makes the encounter resonate with Israel's ancient Gentile adversaries, now coming in supplication. The "dog" language (kynarion, diminutive: house-dog rather than street-dog) was a common Jewish epithet for Gentiles, but the woman's answer accepts the metaphor and finds grace within it: even table-dogs eat the children's crumbs. Her use of the title "Son of David" and "Lord" places her within the orbit of Matthean christological confession despite her Gentile status.
21.literary_structure: The pericope unfolds through three exchanges of increasing theological depth: (1) the woman cries out, Jesus is silent; (2) Jesus explains his Israel-first mandate, the woman kneels; (3) the dog-and-crumbs exchange, the woman's brilliant rejoinder, the healing. Her persistence is not nagging but escalating theological argument — she uses the very boundary Jesus names (children/dogs) to press a claim within it. Matthew heightens the shock of the exchange by giving Jesus the most restrictive statement ("sent only to the lost sheep of Israel," v.24) in all the Gospels, then immediately granting the Gentile woman what she asks on the grounds of her "great faith" — a foil to Israel's "little faith."
21.theological_application: The woman's answer is a model of persistent, theologically intelligent faith: she does not dispute Jesus's statement about the priority of Israel but argues for Gentile inclusion from within the metaphor of the table. "Even the dogs eat the crumbs" is not resignation; it is a claim that the overflow of the children's abundance already reaches her — and that her daughter's healing would not deprive Israel of anything. Jesus's response is commendation: "O woman, great is your faith!" (v.28) — the same formula used for the centurion (Matthew 8:10), another Gentile. The pattern is clear: Gentiles who grasp the logic of the kingdom more deeply than the insiders are the harbingers of the Great Commission.
21.christology: "Son of David" from a Canaanite woman is a remarkable confession: the covenant title for the Messiah of Israel is invoked by the very kind of person who was historically excluded from the covenant assembly (Deuteronomy 23:3; the Canaanites are even further outside). Yet Jesus, who affirmed his Israel-first mandate, grants her request on the basis of her faith. This is not a contradiction of his mission but its deepest logic: the Son of David who comes for Israel's restoration is also the son of Abraham through whom all nations were to be blessed (Genesis 12:3). Her healing is a proleptic fulfillment of the Great Commission before the Cross makes it explicit.

29.pericope_label: Healings at the Sea of Galilee
29.synthesis: Jesus moves from the Tyrian district back to the Sea of Galilee, goes up the mountain, and sits — the teaching posture (v.29). Great crowds come bringing the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others; they place them at his feet and he heals them (v.30). The crowd "marvels" and glorifies the God of Israel (v.31). The summary is brief but loaded: the catalog of healings (lame walking, blind seeing, crippled made whole, mute speaking) is precisely the messianic catalogue of Isaiah 35:5–6 — the same text Jesus cited to John the Baptist in Matthew 11:5. Placing these healings in a Gentile region creates a double fulfillment: Isaiah's promise reaches those outside Israel's borders.
29.historical_context: The phrase "the God of Israel" (v.31) is unusual in Matthew — it appears nowhere else in the Gospel — and likely reflects Gentile voices in a Gentile setting glorifying the God of Israel whom they have just seen work. The location, still in the Decapolis/Gentile vicinity following the Tyre-Sidon journey, matches Mark's account of the healing in the Decapolis (Mark 7:31). The catalog of conditions healed — lame, blind, crippled, mute, and many others (v.30) — echoes both Isaiah 35:5–6 and the messianic signs Jesus listed for John's disciples (Matthew 11:5), confirming that these summary healings are understood as eschatological sign activity.
29.literary_structure: The healing summary bridges the Canaanite woman episode and the Feeding of the Four Thousand, providing a panoramic view of Gentile ministry before the feeding miracle in a Gentile setting. The mountain-sitting posture (v.29) recalls the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1) and the feeding of the five thousand on a hillside — both occasions of authoritative teaching-and-provision. That these healings happen before the disciples point out that the crowd has been with Jesus for three days without food (v.32) deepens the shepherd imagery: he heals, then feeds, as Ezekiel's ideal shepherd was to do for the flock (Ezekiel 34:16).
29.theological_application: "They glorified the God of Israel" (v.31). This single phrase carries enormous weight: Gentile crowds, who had just seen lame people walking and blind people seeing, attributed what they saw not to a wonder-worker or a god of their own tradition but to the God of Israel. The healing ministry of Jesus is not just individual restoration but a public revelation of who God is — the one who wills wholeness, who fulfills prophetic promises, whose messianic agent has brought the signs of the age to come into the present. The Gentiles here do what Nazareth and much of Israel failed to do: recognize and praise the God at work in Jesus.
29.christology: The catalog of healings — lame walking, blind seeing, crippled made whole, mute speaking (vv.30–31) — is the Isaiah 35 messianic program enacted en masse in Gentile territory. The one who sits on the mountain while the crowds bring their broken to his feet is not merely a healer but the fulfillment of Israel's prophetic expectations for the restoration of creation. The God of Israel glorified in v.31 is the Father whose Son performs these works — and the Gentile glorification previews the worldwide worship that will follow the Great Commission of Matthew 28:19.

32.synthesis: After three days in the Gentile region Jesus has compassion on the crowd and refuses to dismiss them fasting lest they collapse on the way home (v.32). The disciples ask how they can find enough bread in the wilderness; Jesus asks what they have — seven loaves and a few fish (vv.33–34). He commands the crowd to sit on the ground, takes the seven loaves and fish, gives thanks, breaks and gives to the disciples who distribute to four thousand men (plus women and children), with seven baskets of fragments remaining (vv.36–38). Then he dismisses the crowd, boards a boat, and comes to Magadan (v.39). The second wilderness feeding, occurring in Gentile territory with different numbers, is not a variant of the first but a deliberate doublet: the bread of the kingdom extends to all peoples.
32.historical_context: Seven loaves, seven baskets, four thousand — the number seven in Jewish tradition signifies fullness and completion, and the Gentile covenant resonance is reinforced: the table of showbread in the tabernacle had twelve loaves (for twelve tribes); seven may evoke the seven nations of Canaan now included, or the seventy Gentile nations of Jewish cosmology (Genesis 10). The word for "basket" differs: kophinos in the five-thousand feeding (a distinctively Jewish wicker basket), spyris here (a large Gentile-style basket used in Acts 9:25). This linguistic difference was noticed by Jesus himself in Matthew 16:9–10 — the two feedings are distinct and both memorable. Magadan (v.39) is probably Magdala on the west shore, returning Jesus to Jewish territory.
32.literary_structure: The doublet structure — five loaves/five thousand/twelve baskets; seven loaves/four thousand/seven baskets — is precisely what Jesus uses in Matthew 16:9–10 to rebuke the disciples' anxiety about bread: they have witnessed both feedings and still do not understand. The compassion motivation ("I have compassion on the crowd," v.32) is identical to the five-thousand feeding (Matthew 14:14) — the Ezekiel 34 shepherd-imagery runs through both. The disciples' bewilderment ("where would we get enough bread?") after having witnessed the first feeding is not stupidity but a narrative device highlighting that the feeding cannot be reduced to a learned technique: each act of provision is an act of Jesus, requiring fresh surrender of what is in hand.
32.theological_application: The compassion that motivates the feeding is explicitly physical: "I am unwilling to send them away hungry, lest they faint on the way" (v.32). The kingdom does not care only for souls. Three days without food is a bodily crisis, and Jesus addresses it as one. The disciples' logistics question — "where is there enough bread in this desolate place?" — is realistic; Jesus's answer is not a logistics plan but an action: take what you have, bring it here. The seven loaves are sufficient because Jesus blesses and breaks them, not because seven is a lot. Discipleship characterized by this feeding is one in which the practitioner brings what is insufficient, trusts the blessing, and distributes what Jesus multiplies.
32.christology: The second wilderness feeding for a Gentile crowd completes the arc begun with the Canaanite woman: the bread of the kingdom, which Jesus came to give first to Israel, reaches the Gentiles by the same action — compassion, broken bread, distribution through the disciples. The seven baskets remaining signal abundance in Gentile territory as surely as the twelve baskets signaled it in Jewish territory. The one who has compassion on both — who blesses and breaks for both — is the Son of Man who will gather from all nations at the harvest, the shepherd of Ezekiel 34:23 who tends not only Israel's scattered sheep but all who hunger and follow.
